teaching.title: The C Program Skeleton
teaching.analogy: A C program is like a recipe card with strict requirements — it MUST start with ingredients list (#include), have a main kitchen (main function), and every instruction ends with a semicolon. Skip any part and the recipe fails!
teaching.content: Every C program has the same skeleton: #include directives at the top, then the main() function where execution begins, and return 0; at the end to signal success. This structure is non-negotiable.

examples.title: Minimal C Program
examples.explanation: This is the smallest valid C program. Every part is necessary.

examples.title: Python vs C
examples.explanation: C requires much more explicit structure than Python.

teaching.funFact: C was created in 1972 by Dennis Ritchie at Bell Labs. The Unix operating system itself was rewritten in C — making C the mother of modern computing! 🖥️

teaching.title: printf() — C's Output Function
teaching.analogy: printf() is C's megaphone. The 'f' stands for 'formatted' — because unlike Python's print(), C's printf() needs you to specify the FORMAT of every value you print using special codes called format specifiers.
teaching.content: printf() prints text to the screen. String text goes in double quotes. Use \n to go to a new line. To print variables, use format specifiers like %d for integers and %s for strings.

examples.title: Basic printf
examples.explanation: \n is the newline character — like pressing Enter.
examples.code:
printf("Hello World\n");  // Hello World + new line
printf("No newline");      // stays on same line
printf("Line 2\n");       // Line 2

examples.title: printf with Variables
examples.explanation: Format specifiers tell printf what type of variable to print.

teaching.funFact: printf() stands for 'print formatted'. The % format specifiers are what make it powerful — %d, %f, %s, %c each handle a different data type! 🎯

teaching.title: #include & return 0
teaching.analogy: #include is like bringing a toolbox into the kitchen before you cook. Without it, the tools (functions like printf) don't exist. return 0 is like signing off — telling the OS the program finished successfully.
teaching.content: #include <stdio.h> gives access to input/output functions like printf and scanf. The 'h' means header file. return 0 in main() tells the operating system the program ended without errors.

examples.title: Headers & Return
examples.explanation: Always include what you need. Always return from main.

teaching.funFact: In C, return 0 is a signal to the OS. Scripts and programs check this 'exit code'. return 1 or higher means something went wrong — powerful for automation! 🤖

mcq.q: Which line gives C access to printf() and scanf()?
mcq.explanation: Header Intel: #include <stdio.h> loads the Standard Input/Output library, which contains printf(), scanf(), and other essential functions. Without it, those functions don't exist.

mcq.q: Where does a C program always start executing?
mcq.explanation: Entry Point: C always starts at main(). No matter how many functions you write, execution begins inside main() and flows from there.

mcq.q: What does 'return 0;' mean in main()?
options: Signal to the OS that the program finished successfully
mcq.explanation: Exit Code: return 0 tells the operating system the program completed without errors. Non-zero return values indicate errors — used in shell scripts and automation.

mcq.q: How do you print a new line in printf()?
mcq.explanation: Escape Sequence: \n is the newline escape sequence. It moves the cursor to the next line. \t is tab, \\ is a backslash. C uses these escape sequences in strings.

mcq.q: Which is the correct C output statement?
mcq.explanation: C Output: printf() is C's standard output function from stdio.h. Unlike Python's print() or Java's System.out.println(), C requires \n explicitly for new lines.

coding.title: The Required Header
coding.objective: What header gives access to printf()? Fill in the library name.
coding.hint: Standard Input/Output — the .h means header file.

coding.title: Add the Newline
coding.objective: What escape sequence moves to a new line inside printf()?

coding.title: Return Success
coding.objective: What value does main() return to signal successful completion?
coding.hint: Zero means success, non-zero means error.

options: Missing #include <stdio.h> — printf is undefined without it
debugging.answer: Missing #include <stdio.h> — printf is undefined without it
debugging.hint: printf() lives in stdio.h. Without the #include, the compiler doesn't know what printf is.

options: Missing semicolon after printf() — every C statement ends with ;
debugging.answer: Missing semicolon after printf() — every C statement ends with ;
debugging.hint: In C, every statement ends with a semicolon ; including printf calls.

options: main() should return int, not void — use: int main()
Hi should be in single quotes
debugging.answer: main() should return int, not void — use: int main()
debugging.hint: C's main() must have return type 'int' because it returns an exit code to the OS.

prediction.explanation: printf() without \n stays on the same line. All three printf calls print on one line: 'C is powerful'.
Each printf has \n which moves to a new line. Output is two separate lines: Line 1 then Line 2.

teaching.title: Variables in C — Always Declare First
teaching.analogy: In C, declaring a variable is like booking a hotel room. You specify the room type (int, float, char) and room number (variable name) BEFORE you check in (assign a value). No booking = no room!
teaching.content: C requires you to declare the type of every variable before using it. The format is: type name = value; or type name; then assign later. In classic C (C89), declarations must come BEFORE any statements in a block.

examples.title: C Variable Declarations
examples.explanation: Type must come first, always.

examples.title: Python vs C Variables
examples.explanation: C is explicit about types — Python infers them.

teaching.funFact: In C89/C90, ALL variable declarations must be at the TOP of a function block — before any executable statements. C99 and later relaxed this rule. Many old codebases still use C89 style! 📜

teaching.title: Naming Rules & Reassignment
teaching.analogy: C variable names are like street addresses — must be unique, start with a letter or underscore, no spaces, no special characters. C convention uses snake_case: player_score, max_health, is_active.
teaching.content: Rules: start with letter or _, no spaces, case sensitive. C convention is snake_case (underscores between words). You can reassign without re-stating the type. Type never changes after declaration.

examples.title: C Naming Convention
examples.explanation: C uses snake_case — different from Java's camelCase.

examples.title: Case Sensitivity
examples.explanation: C treats uppercase and lowercase as different.

teaching.funFact: C variables that start with an underscore and uppercase letter (like _Score) are reserved for the C standard library. Avoid using them in your own code! ⚠️

mcq.q: Which is the correct C variable declaration?
mcq.explanation: C Syntax: int declares the type, score is the name, 100 is the value, ; ends the statement. You cannot skip the type in C.

mcq.q: Which follows C's naming convention?
mcq.explanation: C Convention: C traditionally uses snake_case — all lowercase with underscores between words: player_score, max_health. Java uses camelCase, C uses snake_case.

mcq.q: After 'int x = 5;', can you write 'x = 3.7;'?
options: Compile error — can't assign float to int directly
mcq.explanation: Type Warning: Assigning 3.7 to an int truncates the decimal — x gets 3, not 3.7. C may warn but won't always error. This is a common source of silent bugs!

mcq.q: What does 'int lives; lives = 3;' do?
options: Declares lives first (undefined value), then assigns 3
mcq.explanation: Separate Steps: In C you can declare without initializing, then assign later. But be careful — uninitialized variables have garbage values in C, not 0!

mcq.q: Is 'Score' the same variable as 'score' in C?
options: No, C is case sensitive — they are different
Only if on the same line
mcq.explanation: Case Intel: C is fully case sensitive. Score, score, and SCORE are three completely different variable names.

coding.title: Integer Declaration
coding.objective: What type keyword declares a whole number variable in C?
coding.hint: Same as Java — int for integers.

coding.title: Float Declaration
coding.objective: What C type stores decimal numbers with single precision?
coding.hint: 4 bytes, single precision decimal — shorter than double.

coding.title: Reassign Only
coding.objective: After 'int score = 0;', reassign score to 50 (no type keyword).
coding.hint: Just use the variable name — no type needed for reassignment.

options: Missing type declaration — should be: int x = 42;
debugging.answer: Missing type declaration — should be: int x = 42;
debugging.hint: In C, every variable must be declared with its type before first use.

options: Wrong format specifier — use %f for float, not %d
debugging.answer: Wrong format specifier — use %f for float, not %d
debugging.hint: %d is for integers (int). For float/double, use %f.

prediction.explanation: x = 10, y = 3. x + y = 13. %d prints it as integer. Output: 13.
100 - 30 = 70, then 70 - 20 = 50. Each line reassigns hp. printf with %d prints the integer.

teaching.title: C's Core Data Types
teaching.analogy: C's types are like different-sized boxes: int is a medium box (4 bytes), char is a tiny box (1 byte, one character), float is a glass container for decimals (4 bytes), double is an extra-large glass container (8 bytes, more precision).
teaching.content: C's 4 fundamental types: int (whole numbers), float (single-precision decimals), double (double-precision decimals), char (single character). C has NO built-in boolean or String type — you simulate them!

examples.title: The 4 Core Types
examples.explanation: Each type has a fixed size in memory.
examples.code:
int lives = 3;          // 4 bytes, whole numbers
float speed = 9.8;      // 4 bytes, ~7 decimal digits
double pi = 3.14159265; // 8 bytes, ~15 decimal digits
char grade = 'A';       // 1 byte, single character

examples.title: Type Sizes
examples.explanation: sizeof() returns bytes used by a type.
examples.code:
printf("%lu
", sizeof(int));    // 4
printf("%lu
", sizeof(float));  // 4
printf("%lu
", sizeof(double)); // 8
printf("%lu
", sizeof(char));   // 1

teaching.funFact: C has NO boolean type by default! Programmers use int — where 0 means false and any non-zero means true. C99 added <stdbool.h> for proper bool support. 🔢

teaching.title: Format Specifiers — The Key to printf/scanf
teaching.analogy: Format specifiers are like translation codes between C and the screen. When you tell printf '%d', it's saying 'translate this number as an integer'. Get the code wrong and you get garbage output!
teaching.content: Every type has a format specifier: %d for int, %f for float/double, %c for char, %s for char arrays (strings). Use these in printf() to display and scanf() to read values.

examples.title: Format Specifier Table
examples.explanation: Each type has its own code.

examples.title: Controlling Decimal Places
examples.explanation: You can format float output.

teaching.funFact: Using the WRONG format specifier with printf() in C causes 'undefined behavior' — anything can happen, from garbage output to a crash. C does NOT protect you from this! ⚠️

mcq.q: Which format specifier prints an integer in C?
mcq.explanation: Format Intel: %d is the format specifier for int (decimal integer). %f is for float/double, %c for char, %s for strings (char arrays).

mcq.q: What is the size of a char in C?
mcq.explanation: Size Intel: char is 1 byte in C — it holds values 0–255, representing ASCII characters. This is different from Java's 2-byte char (Unicode).

mcq.q: Does C have a built-in boolean type by default?
mcq.explanation: No Bool: Classic C has no boolean type. 0 is treated as false, any non-zero (usually 1) is true. C99 added <stdbool.h> for bool/true/false keywords.

mcq.q: Which C type gives more decimal precision: float or double?
mcq.explanation: Precision Intel: double has DOUBLE the precision of float. float ≈ 7 decimal digits, double ≈ 15 digits. For serious math, always use double.

mcq.q: What does printf("%.2f", 3.14159); output?
mcq.explanation: Float Format: %.2f means print as float with exactly 2 decimal places. 3.14159 rounded to 2 places = 3.14.

coding.title: Print an Integer
coding.objective: What format specifier prints an int with printf()?

coding.title: Print a Float
coding.objective: What format specifier prints a float/double?

coding.title: Print a Character
coding.objective: What format specifier prints a single char?

options: Wrong specifier — score is int, use %d not %f
debugging.answer: Wrong specifier — score is int, use %d not %f
debugging.hint: %f is for float/double. For int, use %d.

options: char uses single quotes: char letter = 'Z';
debugging.answer: char uses single quotes: char letter = 'Z';
debugging.hint: C char values use single quotes 'Z'. Double quotes make it a string literal.

prediction.explanation: Casting float to int truncates (cuts off) the decimal. 7.9 becomes 7 — it does NOT round up.
%.1f rounds to 1 decimal place. 3.75 rounds to 3.8.

teaching.title: scanf() — Reading User Input
teaching.analogy: scanf() is like a delivery driver — it picks up what the user types and delivers it to a specific ADDRESS in memory. You must give it the ADDRESS (using &) of where to store the value, not just the variable name!
teaching.content: scanf() reads input from the keyboard. It uses the same format specifiers as printf(). But critically, for most types you must prefix the variable name with & (the address-of operator) to tell C WHERE in memory to store the value.

examples.title: Basic scanf Usage
examples.explanation: & is required for most types in scanf.

examples.title: Reading Different Types
examples.explanation: Match the format specifier to the variable type.

teaching.funFact: The & in scanf is the 'address-of' operator — one of C's most important concepts. It's the gateway to understanding pointers, which make C incredibly powerful (and dangerous)! 🔓

teaching.title: Why & is Required
teaching.analogy: Without &, you hand the delivery driver a piece of paper with the word 'house'. With &, you hand them the actual GPS coordinates of your house. The driver NEEDS the exact address to deliver the package correctly!
teaching.content: C functions receive copies of values by default. To let scanf modify a variable, you must pass its memory address with &. This is 'pass by reference' — a foundational C concept. Without &, scanf writes to a random memory location (crash!)

examples.title: With vs Without &
examples.explanation: Missing & is a very common beginner mistake in C.

teaching.funFact: char arrays (strings) in C are already addresses — that's why scanf("%s", name) doesn't need &. Arrays and pointers are deeply intertwined in C! 🔗

mcq.q: Which correctly reads an integer from the user in C?
mcq.explanation: Address Operator: scanf("%d", &x) passes the memory address of x. scanf writes the input value TO that address. Without &, it's undefined behavior.

mcq.q: What does the & symbol mean in scanf("%d", &age)?
options: Address-of operator — gives the memory address of age
mcq.explanation: Address-Of: & is the address-of operator. &age gives the memory address where age is stored. scanf needs this address to know WHERE to write the user's input.

mcq.q: When reading a string into a char array, do you need & in scanf?
options: No — char arrays are already addresses
mcq.explanation: Array Address: char name[20] — 'name' already IS a memory address (pointer to first element). So scanf("%s", name) — no & needed. Adding & would be an error!

mcq.q: What format specifier reads a float with scanf()?
mcq.explanation: Float Input: Use %f for reading into float, %lf (long float) for reading into double with scanf. Note: printf uses %f for both, but scanf distinguishes them!

coding.title: Read an Integer
coding.objective: Complete the scanf to read an integer into variable 'age'. What goes before age?
coding.hint: The address-of operator — tells scanf WHERE to store the value.

coding.title: Read a Float
coding.objective: What format specifier reads a float with scanf()?
coding.hint: Same as printf for float — lowercase f.

coding.title: Read a String
coding.objective: Reading a char array — does it need & in scanf? Type yes or no.
coding.hint: Arrays in C are already memory addresses — & is not needed and would be wrong.

options: Missing & — should be scanf("%d", &x); to pass the address
debugging.answer: Missing & — should be scanf("%d", &x); to pass the address
debugging.hint: scanf needs the address of x (&x) to know where to write the user's input.

options: Wrong format specifier — temp is float, use %f not %d
debugging.answer: Wrong format specifier — temp is float, use %f not %d
debugging.hint: %d reads an int. For float, use %f in scanf.

prediction.explanation: scanf reads 7 into x. printf prints x * 2 = 7 * 2 = 14.
scanf reads 4.7 into f. printf with %.0f rounds to 0 decimal places — 4.7 rounds up to 5.

teaching.title: Comments in C
teaching.analogy: Comments are the sticky notes of code — the compiler ignores them completely, but future-you will thank the current-you for writing them. C supports two styles: old-school block comments and modern line comments.
teaching.content: C supports // single-line comments (added in C99) and /* ... */ multi-line block comments. Block comments are the original C style. Single-line // comments are practical for quick notes.

examples.title: Both Comment Styles
examples.explanation: Both work in modern C (C99 and later).

examples.title: Comment Best Practices
examples.explanation: Comment the WHY, not the WHAT.

teaching.funFact: The original C standard (C89) only supported /* */ block comments. The // single-line style was borrowed from C++ and officially added to C in the C99 standard (1999)! 📜

teaching.title: Preprocessor Directives & Constants
teaching.analogy: Preprocessor directives (lines starting with #) are like the chef's prep work before cooking. The #include brings in tools (libraries), and #define creates named constants — all done BEFORE the actual compilation starts.
teaching.content: #include loads library headers. #define creates constants or macros — they are text substitutions done before compilation. Using #define for constants is traditional C style; modern C uses const instead.

examples.title: #include & #define
examples.explanation: Preprocessor directives start with # and have NO semicolon.

examples.title: const vs #define
examples.explanation: Modern C prefers const over #define for typed constants.

teaching.funFact: #define macros are pure text substitution — no type checking at all! This is why modern C prefers 'const' for constants. #define macros can create very tricky bugs if misused. 🐛

mcq.q: Which was the ORIGINAL comment style in C (C89)?
mcq.explanation: History: /* */ block comments are the original C style from 1972. The // single-line comment was added in C99 (1999), borrowed from C++.

mcq.q: What is wrong with: #define MAX_HP 100;
options: #define macros must NOT end with a semicolon
mcq.explanation: No Semicolon: #define is a preprocessor directive, not a C statement. It should NOT end with ; because the ; would become part of the text substitution, causing bugs!

mcq.q: Which correctly includes the math library in C?
mcq.explanation: Include Syntax: #include <math.h> loads the math library header. Angle brackets < > are for standard library headers. Use quotes "file.h" for your own header files.

mcq.q: What does #define PI 3.14 do?
options: Creates a text replacement — everywhere PI appears, it becomes 3.14 before compiling
mcq.explanation: Text Substitution: #define creates a macro. Before compilation, the preprocessor replaces every occurrence of PI in the code with 3.14. No type, no memory — pure text swap.

coding.title: Single Line Comment
coding.objective: What starts a single-line comment in C (C99 style)?
coding.hint: Two forward slashes — same as Java.

coding.title: Define a Constant
coding.objective: Complete the #define macro. Note: NO semicolon at the end.
coding.hint: Just the value — no = sign, no semicolon in #define.

coding.title: Include Math
coding.objective: What header gives access to math functions like sqrt() in C?
coding.hint: It's the math header file — ends with .h

options: Two bugs: #define uses NO = sign and NO semicolon. Correct: #define PI 3.14
debugging.answer: Two bugs: #define uses NO = sign and NO semicolon. Correct: #define PI 3.14
debugging.hint: #define is: #define NAME value — no = and no ; at the end.

debugging.brokenCode: # This is a comment in C
options: C uses // or /* */ for comments, not # like Python
debugging.answer: C uses // or /* */ for comments, not # like Python
debugging.hint: # in C starts a preprocessor directive, not a comment. Use // or /* */ for C comments.

prediction.explanation: DOUBLE(x) is a function-like macro. DOUBLE(5) is replaced with 5 * 2 = 10 before compilation.
The first line is a // comment — compiler ignores it. Only 'Visible' is printed.

teaching.title: ⚠️ BOSS — THE COMPILER
teaching.analogy: The C Compiler is the strictest boss in the game. It checks every semicolon, every format specifier, every & sign. One mistake and it outputs a wall of errors. Show it you've mastered C Level 1!
teaching.content: THE COMPILER has absorbed all of Level 1's C knowledge: program structure (#include, main, return 0), printf/scanf, typed variables, data types (int/float/double/char), format specifiers (%d %f %c %s), and the & address operator. Defeat it to earn your 'First C Code' badge!

examples.title: What you've mastered
examples.explanation: Everything from C Level 1!
examples.code:
✅ #include <stdio.h> + int main() + return 0
✅ printf() with \n and format specifiers
✅ int, float, double, char declarations
✅ %d, %f, %c, %s format specifiers
✅ scanf() with & address operator
✅ // and /* */ comments, #define

teaching.funFact: The C compiler translates your source code directly to machine code — processor instructions your CPU understands. No virtual machine needed. That's why C programs are blazing fast! ⚡

mcq.explanation: 7 * 7 = 49. printf with %d prints the integer result. Output: 49.

options: Wrong format specifier — float needs %f, not %d
mcq.explanation: Type Mismatch: %d is for integers. Using %d with a float causes undefined behavior — you'd get garbage output. Always match the specifier to the type.
Address Missing: scanf needs &n (address of n) to write the input. Without &, it tries to write to a random memory address — undefined behavior, likely a crash.

mcq.q: What does '\n' do inside a printf string?
mcq.explanation: Newline: \n is the newline escape sequence. It moves the cursor to the beginning of the next line — equivalent to pressing Enter.

mcq.q: What is the return type of main() in C?
mcq.explanation: Return Type: main() in C returns int. return 0 signals success to the OS. return 1 (or other non-zero) signals an error. The OS and shell scripts use this exit code.

coding.title: Boss Challenge: Complete printf
coding.objective: Print integer variable 'score' using printf. What format specifier?
coding.hint: %d is the format specifier for integers.

coding.title: Boss Challenge: Fix the scanf
coding.objective: Reading an integer into variable 'age'. What is missing before age?
coding.hint: The address-of operator — tells scanf where in memory to write.

debugging.hint: Every C statement ends with ; — including variable declarations.

prediction.explanation: printf with three %d specifiers prints a=5, b=3, a+b=8. Output: '5 3 8'.
